skill.name: when-reviewing-pull-request-orchestrate-comprehensive-code-revie
skill.description: Orchestrates a comprehensive pull request review across automated checks, specialist analysis, integration review, and final merge recommendation. The workflow coordinates code quality, security, performance, architecture, documentation, testing, and deployment reviewers.
skill.summary: Coordinate multi-agent pull request reviews with structured gates and final recommendations.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives caused by markdown examples, sample findings, and Graphviz labels rather than executable hidden code. The remaining risk is moderate: copyable shell snippets run project commands, call GitHub CLI, and build temporary file paths from PR inputs.

medium_findings.title: Copyable shell snippets use unvalidated PR arguments
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The workflow includes bash snippets that accept PR_NUMBER and PR_ID, then use them in GitHub CLI commands and temporary file paths. This is dangerous if users copy the snippets into scripts and pass untrusted values, because malformed input can alter command arguments or write unexpected files. Confidence: 0.78. Confidence reasoning: The snippets are documentation, not installed executable code, but the argument flow is explicit and copyable.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The snippets are documentation, not installed executable code, but the argument flow is explicit and copyable.

medium_findings.title: Workflow encourages running project scripts from PR context
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The workflow tells reviewers to run npm, pytest, rspec, lint, test, coverage, and build commands while reviewing a pull request. This is legitimate for code review, but project scripts from an untrusted branch can execute arbitrary local code. Confidence: 0.74. Confidence reasoning: The command intent is clearly review automation, but the operational risk is real when reviewing untrusted contributions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command intent is clearly review automation, but the operational risk is real when reviewing untrusted contributions.

low_findings.title: Static high-severity sensitive-data hits are sample review findings
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The lines mention hardcoded API keys, environment variables, SQL injection, and security issues as examples of findings a reviewer should report. They do not access local secrets, read environment files, or expose credentials. Confidence: 0.94. Confidence reasoning: The surrounding context is sample output and review guidance, not executable code or secret collection logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is sample output and review guidance, not executable code or secret collection logic.

low_findings.title: Graphviz weak-crypto matches are label false positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The process diagram contains workflow labels such as Start and System Architect, which triggered weak-cryptography matching by name only. No cryptographic algorithm or crypto implementation appears in the diagram. Confidence: 0.96. Confidence reasoning: The cited lines are Graphviz labels and comments with no executable behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Graphviz labels and comments with no executable behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell commands in markdown examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes bash examples for claude-flow, npm, pytest, rspec, jq, and GitHub CLI. These are expected for a pull request review workflow but should be run only in an isolated checkout. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The commands are plainly present in fenced bash blocks and are part of the intended workflow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly present in fenced bash blocks and are part of the intended workflow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Temporary files derived from PR identifier
dangerous_patterns.description: The final decision example writes and reads files under /tmp using PR_ID. Users should sanitize or map PR identifiers before using this pattern in real scripts. Confidence: 0.80. Confidence reasoning: The file paths are explicit, but the skill presents them as examples rather than shipped automation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file paths are explicit, but the skill presents them as examples rather than shipped automation.

dangerous_patterns.title: GitHub CLI can publish review comments and labels
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow uses gh commands to request changes, approve pull requests, and edit labels. This is expected behavior, but it can modify repository state if run with a privileged token. Confidence: 0.82. Confidence reasoning: The cited commands explicitly call gh pr review and gh pr edit.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited commands explicitly call gh pr review and gh pr edit.

content.user_title: Review Pull Requests with Multi-Agent Checks
content.value_statement: Large pull requests are hard to review consistently across quality, security, performance, and deployment risk. This skill provides a structured workflow that coordinates specialist reviewers and produces an evidence-based merge recommendation.
actual_capabilities: Defines a four-phase pull request review process with automated checks, specialist reviews, integration analysis, and final approval.
Coordinates reviewer roles for code quality, security, performance, architecture, documentation, dependencies, testing, and deployment.
Provides memory key patterns for storing metadata, phase results, specialist findings, and final summaries.
Includes example commands for claude-flow orchestration, test execution, coverage checks, build checks, and GitHub review updates.
Supplies structured review output formats for findings, severity counts, author comments, and merge recommendations.
Includes a process diagram that visualizes gates, reviewer phases, review loops, and GitHub notification flow.
limitations: The workflow is guidance and examples; it does not provide a packaged executable review tool.
Command snippets need validation and adaptation before use in a real repository.
It assumes claude-flow, GitHub CLI, project test tools, and specialist agents are available.
The four-hour process may be too heavy for small or low-risk pull requests.

use_cases.title: Standardize critical feature reviews
use_cases.description: Use the workflow to apply consistent quality gates before merging large feature pull requests.

use_cases.title: Coordinate security-sensitive reviews
use_cases.description: Assign a security-focused reviewer while keeping performance, testing, and architecture feedback aligned.

use_cases.title: Prepare release-blocking decisions
use_cases.description: Collect automated check results and specialist findings into a clear approve, request changes, or reject recommendation.

prompt_templates.title: Review one pull request
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review PR 123 in owner/repo. Run the four phases and summarize blocking issues first.

prompt_templates.title: Focus on security and tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this pull request with extra attention to authentication, input validation, dependency risk, and test coverage.

prompt_templates.title: Compare review dimensions
prompt_templates.prompt: Orchestrate the review and group findings by security, performance, architecture, tests, documentation, and deployment impact.

prompt_templates.title: Produce a merge decision
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the full workflow, identify cross-phase conflicts, assess rollback risk, and provide an approve, request changes, or reject decision.

output_examples.input: Review PR 312 for a GraphQL API change.
output: Blocking issues: authentication verification, two N+1 query risks, and missing migration guidance.
Recommended next step: fix blocking issues, add a feature flag, and rerun automated checks.

output_examples.input: Summarize specialist findings for a security patch.
output: Security review: fix is appropriate and regression tests cover the vulnerable path.
Deployment review: low risk because no schema or configuration changes are required.
Decision: approve after all automated checks pass.

output_examples.output: The review thanks the contributor, lists blocking issues with file references, separates recommendations, and gives clear next steps.

best_practices: Run copied command examples in an isolated checkout with limited credentials.
Validate pull request identifiers before using them in shell commands or file paths.
Keep final recommendations tied to specific findings, severity, and merge impact.
anti_patterns: Do not run untrusted pull request scripts on a workstation with broad secrets or production access.
Do not treat every specialist comment as blocking without severity calibration.
Do not skip the integration phase when a change affects deployment, data, or public APIs.

faq.question: Does this skill automatically review a pull request?
faq.answer: It provides an orchestration workflow and examples. Users must connect the required tools and agents.

faq.question: Which tools does it assume?
faq.answer: The examples reference claude-flow, GitHub CLI, jq, and project test or build commands.

faq.question: Is it safe for open source pull requests?
faq.answer: Use isolation for untrusted branches. Test and build commands from a pull request can execute local code.

faq.question: Can teams customize the reviewer roles?
faq.answer: Yes. The role list is a template and can be adapted to the repository risk profile.

faq.question: What output should reviewers expect?
faq.answer: The workflow produces categorized findings, severity counts, integration notes, and a final merge recommendation.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: Most static alerts are false positives, but the workflow includes copyable shell and GitHub CLI commands that need safeguards.
